summary: Austrian-born philosopher of science. Proposed falsifiability as the criterion distinguishing science from non-science in The Logic of Scientific Discovery. Attacked Plato, Hegel, and Marx as proto-totalitarians in The Open Society and Its Enemies. Coined 'verisimilitude' in Conjectures and Refutations. Called his philosophy 'critical rationalism.'

works.name: The Logic of Scientific Discovery & Falsifiability
works.description: Popper's major work (1934, originally Logik der Forschung), which proposes falsifiability as the solution to the demarcation problem — distinguishing science from non-science. The book argues that scientific theories should be 'risky and prohibitive,' making 'bold' predictions that could be falsified. It rejects verifiability and induction, accepting Hume's critique of induction. The black swan example demonstrates falsifiability: seeing a black swan falsifies 'all swans are white.' The book outlines a four-step process of deduction and introduces 'basic statements' as claims about observable events. Popper also pioneered the propensity interpretation of probability and argued that Marxism and Freudianism are pseudoscientific.

clues.clue: falsifiability — criterion for distinguishing science from non-science (demarcation problem)
examples: distinguished scientific statements from non-scientific statements by their falsifiability
this thinker formulated the falsifiability criterion to distinguish between science and non-science

clues.clue: black swan example — finding one falsifies 'all swans are white'
examples: seeing an indefinite number of white swans does not justify the conclusion that black swans do not exist
demonstrated with the finding of a black swan proving that every swan is not white

clues.clue: rejected verifiability; accepted Hume's critique of induction — science is deductive, not inductive
examples: rejected verifiability as a suitable criterion of demarcation
Accepting the Humean critique of induction made this author unique among his contemporaries

clues.clue: Marxism and Freudianism are pseudoscientific — fail to make 'bold' predictions
examples: he argued that both Marxist theories of history and Freudian psychoanalysis were pseudoscientific

clues.clue: four-step process of deduction
examples: proposed distinguishing the existential and logical components of a statement as the second step of his four-step process of deduction

clues.clue: propensity interpretation of probability — probabilities are properties of experiments
examples: he argued that probabilities are properties of experiments, pioneering the propensity interpretation of probability

clues.clue: basic statements — theories as claims about the existence of observable events
examples: this thinker characterized theories as claims about the existence of observable events, which he called 'basic statements'

clues.clue: Paul Feyerabend criticized Popper's deductive view; wrote Against Method
examples: Paul Feyerabend criticized this thinker's view that science is deductive, not inductive

clues.clue: Harry Blackmun quoted falsifiability in Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals (1993)
examples: Harry Blackmun quoted the claim that science should be defined by falsifiability

clues.clue: Novalis epigraph: 'Theories are nets: Only he who casts will catch'
examples: Popper's epigraph to The Logic of Scientific Discovery is the quotation 'Theories are nets: Only he who casts will catch' by this German romantic philosopher

works.name: The Open Society and Its Enemies
works.description: Popper's political philosophy work attacking totalitarianism's intellectual roots. Volume I, 'The Spell of Plato,' argues Plato's idealized politics are 'fundamentally identical' to totalitarianism. Volume II, 'The High Tide of Prophecy,' attacks Marx and Hegel. The book argues against historicism (the idea that history unfolds according to inevitable laws) and advocates 'piecemeal engineering' — small, gradual reforms rather than utopian revolution. Popper emphasized minimizing suffering on utilitarian grounds. It also traces a process from 'naive monism' to 'critical dualism.'

clues.clue: Vol. I 'The Spell of Plato' — Plato's politics are 'fundamentally identical' to totalitarianism
examples: this book asserts that Plato's idealized politics are 'fundamentally identical' to totalitarianism

clues.clue: Vol. II 'The High Tide of Prophecy' — attacks Marx and Hegel
examples: this philosopher claimed that Platonic ideas were sympathetic with totalitarianism in a book that attacks Marx and Hegel

clues.clue: against historicism — no society can know its own future
examples: he criticized the idea that history unwaveringly plays itself out in particular patterns

clues.clue: 'piecemeal engineering' — small gradual reforms vs. utopian revolution
examples: a work by him argues for strategies of social improvement that seek to combat urgent evils rather than trying to create a Utopian good; he called this 'piecemeal engineering'

clues.clue: naive monism to critical dualism — natural vs. normative laws
examples: a book by this philosopher describes that people first held 'naive monism,' in which no distinction is made between natural and normative laws

clues.clue: emphasized minimizing suffering on utilitarian grounds
examples: this thinker emphasized minimizing suffering in public policy on utilitarian grounds

works.name: Conjectures and Refutations & Verisimilitude
works.description: Popper's 1963 work introducing the concept of verisimilitude (truthlikeness) — the idea that scientific knowledge grows by getting closer to the truth. He derived a formula: Vs(a) = CtT(a) - CtF(a), where a is a given statement. He adapted Alfred Tarski's work on truth to develop this concept. The idea has 'fallen into disrepute' due to confusion with probability (per Ilkka Niiniluoto).

clues.clue: verisimilitude / truthlikeness — scientific knowledge grows by getting closer to truth; formula Vs(a) = CtT(a) - CtF(a)
examples: coined this term to describe the process by which scientific knowledge grows
this philosopher defined quantitative verisimilitude by means of the formula Vs(a)=CtT(a)-CtF(a)

clues.clue: adapted Alfred Tarski's thought on truth
examples: this thinker adapted Alfred Tarski's thought in his application of a concept derived from the logical content of statements

clues.clue: confused with probability, leading to 'disrepute' (Ilkka Niiniluoto)
examples: the confusion of this property with 'probability' has led to it 'falling into disrepute'

works.name: The Poverty of Historicism & Three Worlds
works.description: Popper's attack on historicism — the idea that history follows inevitable patterns or 'trends.' The book distinguishes trends from laws and argues for 'piecemeal engineering' over holistic utopian engineering. Peter Turchin's cliodynamics essay 'Towards Cliodynamics' refutes this book. Popper's 1967 lecture proposed three worlds: World 1 (physical), World 2 (mental, from biological evolution), and World 3 (products of human thought). He also used the 'cloud vs. clock' analogy for indeterminism, comparing a swarm of gnats (cloud) to a steady clock.

clues.clue: The Poverty of Historicism — confusing trends with laws; piecemeal vs. utopian engineering
examples: this work asserts that 'the habit of confusing trends with laws...inspired the central doctrines of' the title idea

clues.clue: three worlds — physical (1), mental (2), products of thought (3)
examples: this philosopher claimed that the universe only had one world at its inception, but a second one emerged from biological evolution and a third from human's 'products of thoughts'

clues.clue: cloud vs. clock — swarm of gnats (unpredictable) vs. steady clock (orderly) for indeterminism
examples: this thinker gave the example of a swarm of gnats to represent the unpredictable 'cloud,' which he equated with the more orderly and steady 'clock'

clues.clue: Peter Turchin's cliodynamics refutes The Poverty of Historicism
examples: Turchin's essay 'Towards Cliodynamics' refutes this thinker's book The Poverty of Historicism

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Popper called his philosophy 'critical rationalism.' He was Austrian-born and worked at the University of Canterbury, the London School of Economics, and the University of London. He was knighted in 1965. George Soros was his student. Imre Lakatos extended his work on falsifiability. His 'postscript' consists of three volumes: The Open Universe, Realism and the Aim of Science, and Quantum Theory and the Schism of Physics.

clues.clue: critical rationalism — name for his philosophy
examples: this man, who referred to his philosophy as 'critical rationalism'

clues.clue: Austrian-born; worked at Canterbury, LSE, University of London; knighted 1965
examples: after decades of philosophical research at institutions such as the University of Canterbury, the London School of Economics

clues.clue: teacher of George Soros
examples: this teacher of George Soros

clues.clue: Imre Lakatos extended falsifiability
examples: like Imre Lakatos, this philosopher distinguished science from non-science with the criterion of falsifiability

clues.clue: improbable theories are more scientifically valuable (more informative, more testable)
examples: this man suggested that improbable theories are more scientifically valuable, since they are more informative and thus more testable

comprehensive_summary:
Karl Popper is the Austrian-born philosopher of science who proposed falsifiability as the criterion for distinguishing science from non-science, solving the 'demarcation problem.' In The Logic of Scientific Discovery (1934), he argues that scientific theories should make 'bold,' 'risky' predictions that can be falsified — demonstrated by the black swan example. He rejected verifiability and accepted Hume's critique of induction, arguing science is deductive. He accused Marxism and Freudian psychoanalysis of being pseudoscientific because they failed to make falsifiable predictions. Paul Feyerabend criticized Popper's deductive view and later wrote Against Method.

The Open Society and Its Enemies attacks totalitarianism's intellectual roots. Volume I ('The Spell of Plato') argues Plato's politics are 'fundamentally identical' to totalitarianism; Volume II ('The High Tide of Prophecy') attacks Marx and Hegel. The book advocates 'piecemeal engineering' — small, gradual social reforms — over utopian revolution.

Conjectures and Refutations introduces verisimilitude (truthlikeness), the idea that scientific knowledge grows by approximating truth, with the formula Vs(a) = CtT(a) - CtF(a). The Poverty of Historicism attacks the confusion of trends with laws and advocates piecemeal over holistic engineering. Popper proposed three worlds (physical, mental, products of thought) in a 1967 lecture and used the cloud-vs.-clock analogy for indeterminism.

Popper called his philosophy 'critical rationalism,' worked at the LSE and the University of Canterbury, was knighted in 1965, and taught George Soros. Imre Lakatos extended his work on falsifiability. Harry Blackmun quoted Popper on falsifiability in the Daubert v. Merrell Dow Pharmaceuticals decision (1993).
